name: Copper King Mine
short_description: Historic copper mining site on BLM land near Hecla offering opportunities to find chrysocolla, malachite, and copper minerals on public land with minimal restrictions.
description:
The Copper King Mine is a historic mining location on public BLM land in the Laramie Basin region of southeastern Wyoming. This site was actively worked during Wyoming's copper mining era and remains accessible to recreational rockhounders seeking secondary copper minerals like chrysocolla and malachite that formed in the oxidized zones above original ore deposits. The mine sits at 7128 feet elevation on gently sloping terrain, making it relatively accessible for collectors of varying experience levels. Visitors can explore old tailings piles and exposed rock faces where copper minerals display their characteristic blue-green colors. The location offers a unique combination of mining history and active rockhounding opportunity in a high desert landscape.

The geology of this site reflects typical Basin and Range copper mineralization. Primary copper deposits were emplaced during late Cretaceous and early Tertiary hydrothermal activity, with secondary enrichment creating the vibrant chrysocolla and malachite found in the upper oxide zones. The exposed walls and tailings material provide excellent access to these weathered copper minerals without requiring deep excavation.

The terrain consists of gentle slopes with scattered vegetation typical of the 7000+ foot Wyoming high desert. Rockhounding here is straightforward - no permit required and personal use collection is permitted within standard BLM guidelines. Most collectors visit the tailings areas and exposed outcrop faces where mineral specimens are easiest to locate and extract.
history: The Copper King Mine was developed during Wyoming's copper mining boom of the early 20th century when demand for the metal was high. Secondary copper minerals like chrysocolla and malachite formed through weathering and oxidation of primary copper sulfides, creating the colorful specimens that attract rockhounders today.
tips: Focus your search on the old tailings piles and exposed mine walls where weathered copper minerals display bright blue and green colors - these secondary minerals are easier to spot and extract than fresh ore. Use a small hammer and chisel to carefully separate chrysocolla and malachite specimens from host rock, working slowly to avoid shattering the brittle minerals. Look for specimens in seams and fractures within the tailings material rather than attempting deep excavation - surface collecting is most productive here. Bring plenty of water as the high elevation site offers limited shade and can be dry despite the year-round cooler temperatures.
terrain_notes: Elevation 7128 feet in the high desert with gently sloping terrain and scattered sagebrush. Unpaved road access leads to the mine site with parking available near the old workings. The ground is rocky with some loose talus and mine waste material. Walking distance from parking to productive collecting areas is typically less than a quarter mile.
best_season: June - September. While the high elevation provides cooler temperatures year-round, summer months offer the most reliable access with minimal snow cover. Late spring and early fall are also viable, but winter snowfall can block access.
written_directions: From Hecla, head northwest on County Road 34 for approximately 4 miles. Look for signs or BLM markers indicating the Copper King Mine access. Turn onto the unsigned dirt road and follow it for approximately 1-2 miles to the mine site parking area. The final approach road is rough but passable for passenger vehicles with good clearance.
road_conditions: Rough dirt road with washboard surface. High ground clearance recommended but not required for careful driving.
parking_notes: Informal parking area near the mine workings. Space for approximately 5-10 vehicles. No facilities.
collection_rules: Personal use only. Maximum 25 lbs per day per FLPMA regulations. No commercial collection permitted. No motorized excavation or powered equipment. Removal limited to hand tools. Pack out all waste material and respect the historic site.
rules:
Personal use only
Maximum 25 lbs per day per FLPMA
No commercial collection
No motorized excavation
Hand tools only
Pack out waste material
Respect historic mine structures
nearest_services: Laramie, approximately 25 miles south, has all services including gas, food, lodging, and supplies.
accessibility_notes: Gently sloping terrain with uneven rocky ground typical of a mine site. Walking distances are short from parking to collecting areas. No wheelchair access. Sturdy hiking boots recommended due to loose rocks and old mine debris. The site is accessible to most people with reasonable fitness levels.

faq.question: What minerals can I find at Copper King Mine in Wyoming?
faq.answer: Copper King Mine is known for secondary copper minerals, primarily chrysocolla (blue-green), malachite (banded green), and associated quartz. These minerals formed from weathering of primary copper deposits. Specimens range from small fragments to collectible hand samples. The vibrant colors make these minerals highly desirable for collectors.

faq.question: Do I need a permit to rockhound at Copper King Mine near Hecla?
faq.answer: No permit is required to rockhound at Copper King Mine. It is BLM public land open to recreational collecting. You must follow standard BLM regulations: personal use only, maximum 25 lbs per day, no commercial collection, and hand tools only.

faq.question: How far is Copper King Mine from Laramie, and what is the drive like?
faq.answer: Copper King Mine is approximately 25 miles north of Laramie and 7 miles from the town of Hecla. From Hecla, follow County Road 34 northwest for 4 miles, then take the rough dirt road for 1-2 miles to reach the mine. High ground clearance is recommended for the final approach.

faq.question: Is Copper King Mine a good rockhounding spot for beginners?
faq.answer: Yes, Copper King Mine is beginner-friendly. The site has easy terrain on gentle slopes with short walking distances from parking. Surface collecting on mine tailings is straightforward - no permit required, basic hand tools suffice, and the colorful copper minerals are easy to identify. Families with children ages 8 and up will find it accessible.

faq.question: What is the best season to rockhound at Copper King Mine in Wyoming?
faq.answer: June through September offers the best rockhounding conditions at Copper King Mine. The high elevation (7128 feet) keeps temperatures cooler but still pleasant during summer. Late spring and early fall are also viable, but winter snowfall can block access to the site. Summer provides reliable road conditions and ample daylight.

faq.question: What tools and supplies do I need for rockhounding at Copper King Mine?
faq.answer: For Copper King Mine, bring a small hammer, chisel, and cold chisel to carefully extract chrysocolla and malachite specimens. A geologist's pick is helpful for working tailings. Since the site sits at 7128 feet elevation in an arid area, bring at least 2-3 liters of water per person, sunscreen, and a hat. A small pack is useful for carrying specimens and waste material you pack out.

meta_description: Find chrysocolla, malachite, and copper minerals at Copper King Mine on public BLM land near Hecla, Wyoming. No permit required. Personal use collecting allowed.
